glossary.definition: An approach that treats intelligence as a general capability that can be copied/replicated. It is related to the development of computer systems capable of solving a wide range of tasks in different problem domains and controlling themselves autonomously with their own thoughts, worries, feelings, strengths, weaknesses and predispositions. It attempts to achieve a level of intelligent performance where the system is able to solve a wide range of tasks, including novel tasks, and perform them as well as a human. The approach requires the ability to solve general problems in a non-domain-restricted way, in the same sense that a human can; the ability to solve problems in particular domains and particular contexts with particular efficiency; the ability to use its more generalized and more specialized intelligence capabilities together, in a unified way; the ability to learn from its environment, other intelligent systems, and teachers; and the ability to become better at solving novel types of problems as it gains experience with them.

glossary.definition: The idea of Depth-First Search is represented by its title: “depth” means to go deeper and deeper into the state space graph, while expanding nodes; “first” indicates finding the first path that leads to the search goal node and stopping the operation. Therefore, Depth-First Search starts with the search start node. It generates this node’s descendants/ancestors (depending on the search direction). The algorithm selects one of the generated nodes as the current node and expands it. Then, it again selects the new current nodes from the recently generated nodes and continues the expansion. In this way, it goes deeper and deeper into the state space graph. If we imagine that the state space graph is similar to a tree’s crown, the search start node is located in the tree’s trunk, and the search goal node is located at the end of some branch, then Depth-First Search will explore branches of the tree sequentially, each time going to the ends of these branches. In the implementation of this algorithm, new nodes are added to the left side of the OPEN list. Therefore, this list is a type of data structure called a stack. A stack works according to the “Last in, First Out” principle: the last data element entered is the first to be processed.

In general, there are three main types of machine learning algorithm s (Figure 6): supervised learning; unsupervised learning; reinforcement learning. Supervised learning is also called learning with a teacher. It is a type of explicit learning. Supervised learning is similar to learning letters or numbers in school: the teacher shows and pronounces a letter or a number, he/she repeats this process several times, and, in the end, children can recognise different letters and numbers. According to (Atul, 2019), this type of learning can be characterised as "Train me!". In supervised learning, input data (the training set) already contain labels representing the desired solution or answer (Geron, 2017; Pant, 2019). For example, imagine a dataset representing animals by a set of features (like the existence of tail, height, body shape, etc.). In this case, labels will indicate if a specific data object is a cat, a dog or any other animal. Therefore, supervised learning aims to find a rule (mapping) describing a relationship between input data and output (Geron, 2017). A mathematical model represents this rule. Thus, supervised learning is based on predictive models because it predicts the output for a new data object based on the model trained on the training set. The typical tasks belonging to supervised learning are (Geron, 2017; Pant, 2019): Classification is performed when labels of data objects are presented as categories like spam or not spam, cat or dog, etc. Therefore, each data object belongs to a specific category/class. The machine learning algorithm finds a category/class to which a new data object can be attributed. Regression is carried out when labels of data objects are presented as real values (so they are continuous) as the price of a house, the number of graduating students, etc. Therefore, for a new data object, the machine learning algorithm finds a prediction, for example, the prediction of the possible price of a house, the prediction of the potential number of graduating students, etc. Unsupervised learning is also called learning without a teacher. This type of learning is based on unlabelled data (Geron, 2017; Pant, 2019). We can compare it with a child that has a set of bricks of different colours in front of him. Looking at them, he/she starts dividing them into different groups based on their colours. According to (Atul, 2019), unsupervised learning can be characterised as "I am self-sufficient in learning!". The goal of this learning is to reveal patterns/structure of data. Unsupervised learning provides descriptive models. It is also used for data visualisation. The typical tasks belonging to unsupervised learning are the following: Clustering deals with segmenting data into representative classes called clusters (Jones, 2008; Pant, 2019). Therefore, data objects belonging to a specific cluster share some common characteristics, while characteristics differ between clusters. Usually, the grouping of data objects is based on the distance between points or their similarity metrics. However, clustering algorithms cannot say what each cluster is about; they can only separate similar data points from others (Atul, 2019). Geron (2017) gives the following example of clustering: “For example, say you have a lot of data about your blog’s visitors. You may want to run a clustering algorithm to try to detect groups of similar visitors. At no point do you tell the algorithm which group a visitor belongs to: it finds those connections without your help. For example, it might notice that 40% of your visitors are males who love comic books and generally read your blog in the evening, while 20% are young sci-fi lovers who visit during the weekends, and so on.” Association analysis finds rules that are based on common co-occurring values of data objects (Pant, 2019), for example, running an association rule analysis on your sales logs may reveal that people who purchase barbecue sauce and potato chips also tend to buy steak (Geron, 2017). Dimensionality reduction is associated with finding the most essential features to reduce the original feature set, especially by dropping less important features or merging correlated features (Geron, 2017; Pant, 2019), for example, a car's mileage may be significantly correlated with its age, so the dimensionality reduction algorithm will merge them into one feature that represents the car's wear and tear (Geron, 2017). Table 1 compares supervised and unsupervised learning. Table 1. Supervised learning vs Unsupervised learning Criterion Supervised learning Unsupervised learning Type of learning Explicit (the teacher teaches a child) Implicit (a child is self-sufficient in learning) Task Based on the training data that includes feature values together with labels representing the desired solution/ answer/ output, to infer a mathematical function describing a relationship between input data (feature values) and output To reveal hidden patterns/structure of the given unlabelled data Tasks Classification Regression Clustering Association analysis Dimensionality reduction Type of models Predictive Descriptive Reinforcement learning is reward-based learning. According to (Atul, 2019), it can be characterised as "My life-my rules!". Reinforcement learning is suitable for agents (like robots, self-driving cars, or a game- playing software) who operate in a changing environment and, to act in the environment, they need to find the best sequence of actions that maximise the end goal (Pant, 2019). Therefore, the agent learns how to act in the environment. The agent receives feedback: a reward if it acts correctly and a penalty if it acts incorrectly (Geron, 2017; Pant, 2019). The reward is represented as a numerical value but it is given with some delay, like at the end of the game when it is known who has won the game: computer or human.

glossary.definition: a metric that evaluates a fraction of correct predictions over a given set of data objects, calculated by summing up values of the main diagonal of the confusion matrix and dividing it by the total number of data objects in the dataset
glossary.sentence: Accuracy metric evaluates a fraction of correct predictions over a given set of data objects. It is calculated in the following way (Kubat, 2017) : Accuracy for the multiclass classification is calculated by summing up values of the main diagonal of the confusion matrix and dividing it by the total number of data objects in the dataset.

glossary.definition: a metric that evaluates a fraction of predicted positives that are true positives, calculated by taking the value on the diagonal as true positives and the sum of other values in the column corresponding to the class as false positives, and then summing up precisions for all classes and dividing the sum by the number of classes
glossary.sentence: A precision metric evaluates a fraction of predicted positives that are true positives. In other words, the accuracy of the positive predictions (Geron, 2017) . Precision is the probability that the classifier is right when labelling an example as positive (Kubat, 2017) . It is calculated in the following way (Kubat, 2017; Geron, 2017) : In the multiclass classification task, we first calculate a precision for each class, taking the value on the diagonal as true positives and the sum of other values in the column corresponding to the class as false positives. After that, we calculate the macro average precision for the task by summing up precisions for all classes and dividing the sum by the number of classes.

glossary.definition: a metric (also called sensitivity and true positive rate) that evaluates a fraction of data objects classified as positives out of the total actual positive data objects, calculated by taking the value on the diagonal as true positives and the sum of other values in the row corresponding to the class as false negatives, and then summing up recall values for all classes and dividing the sum by the number of classes in the dataset
glossary.sentence: A recall metric (also called sensitivity and true positive rate) evaluates a fraction of data objects classified as positives out of the total actual positive data objects. According to (Kubat, 2017) , recall is the probability that a positive data object will be correctly recognised by the algorithm. It is calculated in the following way (Kubat, 2017; Geron, 2017) : In the multiclass classification, we first calculate a recall for each specific class, taking the value on the diagonal as true positives and the sum of other values in the row corresponding to the class as false negatives. After that, we calculate the macro average recall for the task by summing up recall values for all classes and dividing the sum by the number of classes in the dataset.

glossary.definition: an alternative name for the recall metric
glossary.sentence: A recall metric (also called sensitivity and true positive rate) evaluates a fraction of data objects classified as positives out of the total actual positive data objects.

glossary.definition: the y-axis of the ROC curve, which plots false positive rate versus true positive rate for each value of the hyperparameter

glossary.definition: the situation where increasing precision reduces recall and vice versa
glossary.sentence: Unfortunately, you can't have it both ways: increasing precision reduces recall and vice versa. This is called the precision/recall tradeoff .

glossary.definition: a metric used in classification tasks that represents the harmonic mean of precision and recall, calculated by macro average precision and recall, lying in the range [0,1]
glossary.sentence: The F1 score is one more metric used in the classification tasks. Thus, it estimates the number of data objects classified correctly. It is calculated in the following way (Chapelle et al., 2006; Geron, 2017) : The value of the F1 score lies in the range [0,1]. In the multiclass classification task, macro F1 can be calculated by the macro average precision and recall.

glossary.definition: a curve that visualises the behaviour of the classifier under different values of its parameters by plotting false positive rate (x-axis) versus true positive rate (y-axis) for each value of the hyperparameter

The ROC curve plots false positive rate (x-axis) versus true positive rate (y-axis) for each value of the hyperparameter, helping you visualise values that would be good choices for the tradeoff (Russell & Norvig, 2010; Geron, 2017; Kubat, 2017) .

glossary.sentence: The inference engine depends on a knowledge representation schema that is used to describe knowledge.

glossary.definition: The process of knowledge being acquired from a domain expert, processed by a specialist called a knowledge engineer and then added to the knowledge base, or the domain expert can describe and store knowledge using an automated knowledge acquisition system, or nowadays intelligent computer systems are able to learn from examples.
glossary.sentence: How does knowledge get to the knowledge base? There are several main methods used for this purpose (Hopgood, 2001): 1) knowledge is acquired from a domain expert, processed by a specialist called a knowledge engineer and then added to the knowledge base, 2) the domain expert can describe and store knowledge using an automated knowledge acquisition system, and 3) nowadays intelligent computer systems are able to learn from examples.

glossary.definition: A specialist who knows well methods and tools of artificial intelligence, as well as who has knowledge how to describe and represent knowledge in the knowledge base.
glossary.sentence: The knowledge engineer is a specialist who knows well methods and tools of artificial intelligence, as well as who has knowledge how to describe and represent knowledge in the knowledge base.

glossary.definition: Anyone who has deep knowledge (of both facts and rules) and substantial practical experience in a particular (usually limited) domain.
glossary.sentence: Anyone can be considered a domain expert if he/she has deep knowledge (of both facts and rules) and substantial practical experience in a particular (usually limited) domain ( Negnevitsky , 2011).

glossary.definition: An automated system that could be used either by the domain expert directly or by the knowledge engineer to describe knowledge and to build a knowledge base, and such systems can offer a variety of user interfaces, for example, they could offer creation of diagrams representing some scenarios from the problem domain or the creation of structured description of knowledge, and such systems include a set of tools used for the analysis and generation of knowledge and checking the consistency of a created representation.
glossary.sentence: The knowledge acquisition system is an automated system that could be used either by the domain expert directly or by the knowledge engineer to describe knowledge and to build a knowledge base. Such systems can offer a variety of user interfaces, for example, they could offer creation of diagrams representing some scenarios from the problem domain or the creation of structured description of knowledge. Moreover, such systems include a set of tools used for the analysis and generation of knowledge and checking the consistency of a created representation.

glossary.definition: Forming high-level conclusions from collections of raw data.
glossary.sentence: Interpretation - forming high-level conclusions from collections of raw data.

glossary.definition: Finding a configuration of system components that meets performance goals while satisfying a set of design constraints.
glossary.sentence: Design - finding a configuration of system components that meets performance goals while satisfying a set of design constraints.

glossary.definition: Devising a sequence of actions that will achieve a set of goals given certain starting conditions and run-time constraints.
glossary.sentence: Planning - devising a sequence of actions that will achieve a set of goals given certain starting conditions and run-time constraints.

glossary.definition: Comparing a system’s observed behaviour to its expected behaviour (for example, instruction-assisting in the education process in technical domains).
glossary.sentence: Monitoring - comparing a system’s observed behaviour to its expected behaviour (for example, instruction-assisting in the education process in technical domains).

glossary.definition: Governing the behaviour of a complex environment.
glossary.sentence: Control - governing the behaviour of a computer environment.

glossary.definition: Schemas that represent knowledge in the form of logical statements, using constants, variables, functions, predicates, logical operators, and apply inference rules that allow getting new true knowledge from a set of true knowledge.
glossary.sentence: logical knowledge representation schemas represent knowledge in the form of logical statements, using constants, variables, functions, predicates, logical operators, and apply inference rules that allow getting new true knowledge from a set of true knowledge;

glossary.definition: A type of knowledge representation where relations are represented explicitly by arcs.
glossary.sentence: However, comparing to the networked knowledge representation schemas, each node in the graph represents not a single concept/object, but a complex structure describing an object/concept together with its properties and parts.

glossary.definition: Schemas that use a graph to represent knowledge, where nodes represent complex structures describing an object/concept with its properties and parts, using a referencing principle instead of explicit arcs for relations.
glossary.sentence: Structured knowledge representation schemas also use a graph to represent knowledge.

glossary.term: procedural or rule-based knowledge representation schemas
glossary.definition: Schemas that represent knowledge in the form of a set of procedures/instructions.
glossary.sentence: procedural or rule-based knowledge representation schemas represent knowledge in the form of a set of procedures/instructions.

glossary.definition: Theories of human mind that were developed in psychology, which postulate that knowledge on different concepts is related by associations in the human mind and these associations define the meaning of a concept in relation to meaning of other concepts.
glossary.sentence: Networked knowledge representation schemas are based on associationist theories of human mind that were developed in psychology. These theories postulate that knowledge on different concepts is related by associations in the human mind and these associations define the meaning of a concept in relation to meaning of other concepts (Luger, 2009), for example, the concept “university” for the most part of students could be associated with such concepts as lectures, students, professors, exams, science etc. All the mentioned concepts define the meaning of the concept “university”.

glossary.definition: A structure used in knowledge representation where nodes represent complex structures.
glossary.sentence: Structured knowledge representation schemas also use a graph to represent knowledge.

glossary.definition: Explicitly used in networked knowledge representation schemas to represent relations.
glossary.sentence: However, comparing to the networked knowledge representation schemas, each node in the graph represents not a single concept/object, but a complex structure describing an object/concept together with its properties and parts.

glossary.definition: A principle used in structured knowledge representation schemas where one node references another node by its name instead of using explicit arcs for relations.
glossary.sentence: Moreover, relations are not represented explicitly by arcs at it was done in semantic networks, but a referencing principle is used when one node references another node by its name.

glossary.definition: An example of structured knowledge representation schemas developed by Marvin Minsky in 1975, based on the idea of selecting a structure called a Frame from memory to adapt to new situations by changing details.
glossary.sentence: Frames are an example of structured knowledge representation schemas. Marvin Minsky developed frames in 1975.

glossary.definition: The developer of frames in 1975.
glossary.sentence: Marvin Minsky developed frames in 1975.

glossary.definition: A situation that an individual has experienced in the past and structured knowledge of which is kept in memory.
glossary.sentence: This means that an individual has experienced some sort of situation in the past.

glossary.definition: A structure used to store knowledge about each object in one place in a structured way.
glossary.sentence: Frames have the following advantages: frames store knowledge about each object in one place in a structured way;

glossary.definition: Entities for which information is relevant, such as institutional/governmental forms.
glossary.sentence: In other words, it is a data structure that represents typical knowledge about an object/concept (Callan, 2003; Negnevitsky , 2011) or, in other words, information relevant to stereotyped entities (Luger, 2009).

glossary.definition: Examples of stereotyped entities in the real world, such as different types of forms in which an individual provides typical information.
glossary.sentence: An example of a frame in the real world is different types of institutional/governmental forms in which an individual provides typical information about him/herself, like name, surname, address, phone number, date of birth, etc.

glossary.definition: An example of a frame given in Figure 1 to demonstrate the idea of a frame.

glossary.definition: A component of the specific structure of a frame that includes titles, slot values, and facets, which can be symbolic, numerical, boolean, empty, or contain a title of another frame.
glossary.sentence: The frame has a specific structure (Fig.2) that includes: slot titles, slot values and facets ( Negnevitsky , 2011).

glossary.definition: An extension to the slot-value structure of frames that represents extended knowledge about slot values and tells the inference engine how to process the property of the object, allowing to set a slot value and control user's access.
glossary.sentence: Facets are an extension to the slot-value structure of frames as they represent extended knowledge about slot values and tell the inference engine how to process the property of the object, allow to set a slot value and control user's access to the slot value ( Negnevitsky , 2011).

glossary.definition: A way slot values can be provided, where many slot values are assigned during the creation of the knowledge base.

glossary.definition: Programs like databases or expert systems that can deliver slot values to intelligent systems.
glossary.sentence: External programs – nowadays intelligent systems communicate with external programs like databases or expert systems that can deliver slot values;

glossary.definition: The possibility to assign procedures and methods to slots that, for example, will calculate a slot value when it is needed.
glossary.sentence: Procedural attachments to frames – it is possible to assign procedures and methods to slots that, for example, will calculate a slot value when it is needed (see further in the text );

glossary.definition: A source that can provide slot values during the system’s operation, as the intelligent system can demand the user to provide slot values.
glossary.sentence: user – the intelligent system can demand the user to provide slot values during the system’s operation;

glossary.definition: The process of acquiring knowledge about an object by checking a corresponding frame and its slots, and applying steps like performing inheritance and checking inherited demons IF-NEEDED.
glossary.sentence: If necessary knowledge is not found, then the inference engine can apply the steps specified below and stop when the necessary knowledge is found: perform inheritance (if it is possible ); check inherited demons IF-NEEDED.

glossary.definition: Collections of related frames linked together.
glossary.sentence: According to Minsky (1975), “collections of related frames are linked together into frame-systems” .

glossary.definition: A type of frame that contains common properties of a set/class/kind/type of objects/concepts, helps to represent a problem in an abstract form, typically includes a title, common properties, and common property values, and can be used to create sub-classes.
glossary.sentence: There are two main types of frames (Jones, 2009; Negnevitsky , 2011; Winston, 1992): A class/generic frame. It contains common properties of a set/class/kind/type of objects/concepts.

glossary.definition: Frames that inherit slots from a class frame and may add new slots.
glossary.sentence: Next, there are two sub-class frames: Cat and Dog . They inherit slots from the Animal class frame, and each of them has new slots added:

glossary.definition: Frames that represent specific objects and are instances of class or sub-class frames.
glossary.sentence: Finally, there are four instance frames: TreeAlex and BrownLucie are instances for the Person class frame; Tom is an instance for the Cat class frame; Rex is an instance for the Dog class frame.

glossary.definition: The relation defined between frames, represented as a Class slot in sub-class frames and instance frames.
glossary.sentence: The generalization relation is defined between TreeAlex-Person , BrownLucie-Person , Tom-Cat , Rex-Dog , Cat-Animal , Dog-Animal . It is represented as a Class slot in the sub-class frames and instance frames.

glossary.definition: A type of relation that allows representing extended knowledge about parts of objects/concepts by associating frames corresponding to the components of an object with a frame representing the whole.
glossary.sentence: Aggregation relation .

glossary.definition: A self-contained piece of computer code written by the developer of an intelligent computer system (Negnevitsky, 2011). We can use such procedures for different purposes like generating an image of the object, calculating some numerical values, such as the amount of taxes to be paid, acquiring data from external sources, etc.
glossary.sentence: In general, it is possible to add regular procedures and/or demons. A regular procedure is a self-contained piece of computer code written by the developer of an intelligent computer system ( Negnevitsky , 2011). We can use such procedures for different purposes like generating an image of the object, calculating some numerical values, such as the amount of taxes to be paid, acquiring data from external sources, etc.

glossary.definition: A procedure that is invoked as a side effect of some other action in the knowledge base (Luger, 2009).
glossary.sentence: Demons implement actions in a frame-based expert system (Jones, 2009; Luger,2009; Mallach, 1994; Negnevitsky , 2011; Winston, 1992). A demon is a procedure that is invoked as a side effect of some other action in the knowledge base (Luger, 2009).

glossary.definition: It is triggered when the slot value is unspecified, and it is necessary to calculate/to set it;
glossary.sentence: The common demons are: IF-NEEDED – it is triggered when the slot value is unspecified, and it is necessary to calculate/to set it;

glossary.definition: It is triggered when a slot value changes;
glossary.sentence: The common demons are: IF-NEEDED – it is triggered when the slot value is unspecified, and it is necessary to calculate/to set it; IF-CHANGED – it is triggered when a slot value changes;

glossary.definition: It is triggered when a slot value is unspecified, and it is added to the frame;
glossary.sentence: The common demons are: IF-NEEDED – it is triggered when the slot value is unspecified, and it is necessary to calculate/to set it; IF-CHANGED – it is triggered when a slot value changes; IF-ADDED – it is triggered when a slot value is unspecified, and it is added to the frame;

glossary.definition: It is triggered when a slot value is deleted.
glossary.sentence: The common demons are: IF-NEEDED – it is triggered when the slot value is unspecified, and it is necessary to calculate/to set it; IF-CHANGED – it is triggered when a slot value changes; IF-ADDED – it is triggered when a slot value is unspecified, and it is added to the frame; IF-REMOVED – it is triggered when a slot value is deleted.

glossary.definition: A high-level frame that serves as a template for sub-class frames and instance frames.
glossary.sentence: First, there are two high-level class frames: Person and Animal .

glossary.definition: Relations between frames, such as Owns or Animal owner, or Afraid of.
glossary.sentence: The frame system also represents the following associative relations between frames: BrownLucie - Tom , the relation Owns , and Tom- BrownLucie , the relation Animal owner; TreeAlex - Rex , the relation Owns , and Rex- TreeAlex , the relation Animal owner; Tom – Rex , the relation Afraid of .

glossary.definition: A knowledge-based intelligent system in which domain knowledge is represented in the form of a set of rules.
glossary.sentence: The most popular expert systems are rule-based systems ( Negnevitsky , 2011). A rule-based expert system is a knowledge-based intelligent system in which domain knowledge is represented in the form of a set of rules (Hopgood, 2001).

glossary.definition: Schemas used to develop rule-based expert systems, representing a set of problem-solving instructions rather than knowledge about objects/concepts.
glossary.sentence: Rule-based or procedural knowledge representation schemas are used to develop such systems. They represent a set of problem-solving instructions not a knowledge about objects/concepts.

glossary.definition: A chunk of knowledge about solving a problem, presented in the way: IF condition THEN consequence.
glossary.sentence: The simplest type of rules is a production rule . Each production rule is a chunk of knowledge about solving a problem (Luger, 2009; Negnevitsky , 2011). A production rule is presented in the following way (Callan, 2003; Hopgood, 2001; Luger, 2005; Mallach, 1994; Negnevitsky , 2011; Winston, 1992): IF condition THEN consequence - action/statement/conclusion

glossary.definition: A rule-based expert system consisting of three parts: a knowledge base containing a set of production rules, a working memory, and an inference engine.
glossary.sentence: Production systems are rule-based expert systems that consist of three parts (Callan, 2003; Luger, 2005; Negnevitsky , 2011): a knowledge base that contains a set ofproduction rules; a working memory; an inference engine.

glossary.definition: Where data most recently entered in the working memory is used by the antecedent of a rule to fire.
glossary.sentence: This method relies on time tags attached to each fact in the working memory; thus, in the conflict set, the expert system first fires the rule whose antecedent uses the data most recently added to the working memory.

glossary.definition: A part of a production rule that can have multiple conditions joined by the logic operators AND, OR or a combination of both.
glossary.sentence: Regarding the parts of condition and consequence, it is worst to remember that ( Negnevitsky , 2011): a rule can have multiple conditions and consequences joined by the logic operators AND, OR or a combination of both;

glossary.definition: A part of a production rule that can have multiple conditions and consequences joined by the logic operators AND, OR or a combination of both.
glossary.sentence: Regarding the parts of condition and consequence, it is worst to remember that ( Negnevitsky , 2011): a rule can have multiple conditions and consequences joined by the logic operators AND, OR or a combination of both;

glossary.definition: A linguistic object that is linked to a value by an operator.
glossary.sentence: the parts of condition and consequence usually incorporates two parts: an object (linguistic object) and its value. An operator (such as is, are, is not, are not, mathematical operators) links a value by mathematical operators;

glossary.definition: If a domain expert first needs to gather some information and then tries to infer from it whatever can be inferred, choose the forward chaining inference engine.
glossary.sentence: However, if your domain expert begins with a hypothetical solution and then attempts to find facts to prove it, choose the backward chaining inference engine.

glossary.definition: If your domain expert begins with a hypothetical solution and then attempts to find facts to prove it, choose the backward chaining inference engine.
glossary.sentence: If a domain expert first needs to gather some information and then tries to infer from it whatever can be inferred, choose the forward chaining inference engine.

glossary.definition: Real domain experts solve problems in a specific domain.
glossary.sentence: Typically, the selection of a search direction is based on the analysis of how real domain experts solve problems in a specific domain.

glossary.definition: The structure and the order of production rules in the knowledge base determine how to search the knowledge base and how quickly it will be searched.
glossary.sentence: The structure and the order of production rules in the knowledge base determine how to search the knowledge base and how quickly it will be searched.

glossary.definition: Conflict resolution is the method of choosing one rule to fire from those that are able to fire, i.e., from the set of enabled rules, known as the conflict set.
glossary.sentence: Conflict resolution is the method of choosing one rule to fire from those that are able to fire, i.e., from the set of enabled rules, known as the conflict set.

glossary.definition: The set of enabled rules, known as the conflict set.
glossary.sentence: Conflict resolution is the method of choosing one rule to fire from those that are able to fire, i.e., from the set of enabled rules, known as the conflict set.

glossary.definition: Rules that are able to fire.
glossary.sentence: Conflict resolution is the method of choosing one rule to fire from those that are able to fire, i.e., from the set of enabled rules, known as the conflict set.

glossary.definition: The part of a rule that uses the data most recently added to the working memory.
glossary.sentence: This method relies on time tags attached to each fact in the working memory; thus, in the conflict set, the expert system first fires the rule whose antecedent uses the data most recently added to the working memory.

glossary.definition: The part of a production rule that can only have one action or statement/conclusion added to it.
glossary.sentence: try adding only one action or statement/conclusion to the THEN part of production rules.

glossary.definition: The control cycle 'Recognize-act'.
glossary.sentence: At each stage of the control cycle "Recognize-act", the selected rule can be displayed.

glossary.definition: Data in the working memory that have time tags attached to them.
glossary.sentence: This method relies on time tags attached to each fact in the working memory; thus, in the conflict set, the expert system first fires the rule whose antecedent uses the data most recently added to the working memory.

glossary.definition: Systems with the advantages of production rules, separation of knowledge and control, modularity of production rules, tracing and explanation, and dealing with incomplete and uncertain knowledge.
glossary.sentence: Rule-based expert systems have the following advantages: Production rules are a natural way of knowledge representation...

glossary.definition: The inference mechanism searches the whole knowledge base in each iteration.
glossary.sentence: in each iteration, the inference mechanism searches the whole knowledge base.

glossary.definition: Attached to each fact in the working memory.
glossary.sentence: This method relies on time tags attached to each fact in the defined working memory; thus, in the conflict set, the expert system first fires the rule whose antecedent uses the data most recently added to the working memory.

glossary.definition: A single 'chunk' of problem-solving knowledge that corresponds to a rule.
glossary.sentence: In addition, because each rule corresponds to a single 'chunk' of problem-solving knowledge, the rule content can provide a meaningful explanation of the system’s current state and action.

glossary.definition: The code for program control that can be altered without changing the set of production rules.
glossary.sentence: The advantages of this separation include ease of modifying the knowledge base without requiring a change in the code for program control and, conversely, the ability to alter the code for program control without changing the set of production rules

glossary.definition: The syntactic independence of rules supports the incremental development of expert systems by successively adding, deleting, or modifying the knowledge (rules) of the system.
glossary.sentence: This syntactic independence supports the incremental development of expert systems by successively adding, deleting, or modifying the knowledge (rules) of the system.

{"domain": "glossary", "glossary": [{"term": "state space graph", "definition": "A data structure organizing and storing data about states and actions of the problem-solving process. It consists of three levels: the first level shows moves from the initial state of the game that can be performed by the player with the X mark; the second level shows moves represented by the nodes for the player with the O mark; and the last level shows moves of the player with the X mark. It is used to represent the game and requires a search algorithm to find a solution path." , "sentence": "So far, we have created three levels of the state space graph: the first level shows the moves from the initial state of the game that can be performed by the player with the X mark; the player with the O mark can perform the moves represented by the nodes in the second level, and the last level in our example again shows the moves of the player with the X mark. The use of alternating moves in deterministic two-player games of perfect information limits us in terms of the creation of the state space graph. We must avoid using any arcs in the state space graph other than those between two adjacent levels, and especially arcs between nodes at the same level or arcs crossing several levels (Figure 7). As you already know, each node level contains states from which only one of the two players can make a move. If an arc was drawn between two nodes at the same level, this would indicate that the same player could make two moves rather than only one, which would contradict the principle of a zero-sum deterministic two-player game of perfect information as defined at the beginning of this topic. However, please remember that these restrictions are valid only for this specific type of games, not for all state space graphs! An intelligent computer system will effectively be able to use the state space graph for the game only if each node in the graph clearly represents a game state at any specific moment in time. As a result, before creating a state space graph, the developer of an intelligent computer system should determine the parameters that characterise the states in the game . These parameters can be described as follows: they are related to a computer implementation of a game; they correspond to the properties of the game and its course, in which the values change after making a move during the course of the game; they are not related to the characteristics of the state space graph, such as the number of nodes, the number of levels, the number of arcs, etc.; the values of the parameters allow the players to track the course of the game, since they determine a specific state in the game at a specific moment in time; the values of the parameters should be represented in each node of the state space graph; the parameters depend on the game, and thus may be very different; for example, they may include the score of each player, the current game board, or both the scores of the players and the current game board, etc. The complexity of a state space graph is a critical aspect. It is vital to reduce the complexity to avoid overconsumption of computational resources. When we represent games, we can avoid multiple representations of the same state within a specific node level. Nodes at the same level are identical if they have the same values for all of the parameters that characterise the states of the game. If there are identical nodes at the same level, they are not displayed several times, but only once per level, in order to save computational resources. In this case, several incoming arcs are drawn for a single node. Let’s consider the example of the construction of a state space graph for the following oversimplified game. A numerical string “2-2-2-3-3” is given at the beginning of the game. The alternate score of the players is 8:8, and the players make alternate moves. During a move, a player removes any number from the numerical string and subtracts it from his or her initial score. The game stops when only one number is left in the numerical string, and the player who has the highest score wins the game. If the score is the same for both players, the result is a draw. In this game, we have three parameters that characterise states: the numerical string, the score of the first player, and the score of the second player. Hence, three values should be represented in each node of the state space graph. The root node contains the initial numerical string and a score of 8 for each player (Figure 8). The first player starts the game. There are two legal moves from the root node: to remove a value of 2 from the string and to get a score of 6; to remove a value of 3 from the string and to get a score of 5. We now have two new states, and need to consider the legal moves from each state. It is now the turn of the second player. Two moves are possible from each state, and as a result, four new states are generated. We can continue in the same way until we reach leaf nodes, in which there is only one number left in the numerical string. The state space graph of the game also represents a data structure. To allow an intelligent computer system to play the game, we need to implement a search algorithm. Topic 2.3 - State Space Search The last topics have explained how humans solve problems and how a problem-solving process is represented in an intelligent computer system by using a state space graph. However, when solving a problem, an individual, based on his/her own subjective criteria, chooses the most appropriate problem-solving alternative from all possible alternatives and performs actions included in this alternative to find a solution to the problem. Therefore, a logical question would be – what else does an intelligent computer system need to be able to find a solution to the problem if it already has a formal description of the problem-solving process? In other words, how can we ensure that an intelligent computer system chooses only one problem-solving alternative from all the alternatives represented in the state space graph? It means that it is not enough to provide an intelligent computer system with a state space graph. It also needs a search algorithm. Therefore, an intelligent computer system needs a state space graph to be able to solve problems. It is a data structure organizing and storing data about states and actions of the problem-solving process. Additionally, an intelligent computer system needs a search algorithm that processes the data structure to find a solution to the problem based on known input data (Figure 1). A task of any search algorithm is to find a solution path if such a path exists in the state space graph (Luger, 2009) . To be able to do that, the search algorithm needs to know a search start node and a search goal node, in addition to which, while searching for a solution, it should process a data structure – a state space graph (Figure 2). A search start node is a specific node in the state space graph that serves as a starting point for searching for a solution. A search goal node is a particular node in the state space graph that should be reached while searching for a solution. A solution path is a path through the state space graph from a given search start node to a given search goal node. It corresponds to an ordered sequence of actions that lead to the solution to the problem. There could be more than one solution path because the same search goal node can be reached by different paths that start at the same search start node (the fragment is based on information given in (Luger, 2009)) The explanations given above are represented in Figure 3. Let’s return to our example of the robot that moves in a restricted environment from an entrance to either of two exits (Figure 4). Imagine a situation when the robot needs to reach a specific exit. Therefore, the search start node is C1. The search goal node is B3. One of the solution paths is C1->B1->B2->B3, or, taking into account that graph arcs represent actions of the problem-solving process, we get a robot motion trajectory - the robot should move left-right-straight ahead. Another solution path is C1->C2->B2->B3 - the robot should move straight ahead-left-right." , "language": "en"}, {"term": "search algorithm", "definition": "A component needed by an intelligent computer system to find a solution to the problem based on known input data. Topic 4.5 - Basics of Artificial Neural Networks Nowadays, artificial neural networks are experiencing their heyday. Few of us have not heard of the concept of deep learning, which is closely related to artificial neural networks. Artificial neural networks have found application in various fields: text classification and categorization, student performance modelling, character recognition, credit card attrition, video surveillance, stock market prediction, cancer cell analysis, vehicle scheduling, etc. Moreover, artificial neural networks can be used for carrying out both supervised and unsupervised machine learning tasks. This topic will look mainly at artificial neural networks, which can be used for the classification task of supervised machine learning. According to (Jones, 2009), “neural networks are biologically motivated computing structures that are conceptually modeled after the brain.” Therefore, to understand their operational principles, we need to focus on: the structure of the biological neuron; the structure of an artificial neuron and its similarity to the biological neuron; the operational principles of a single artificial neuron; the operational principles of artificial neural networks. Biological neuron The human brain consists of cells called neurons. Each neuron has a cell body (a soma) containing a nucleus and two types of root-like extensions from the cell body: many dendrites and a single axon (Fig. Dendrites look like a little brush and serve for receiving signals from other neurons. The axon itself eventually splits into a number of branches, each terminating in a little endbulb that comes close to the dendrites of another neuron.
